coredata.dc:title: Versatile hydrogel drug carrier loading with peptide-carbon dots conjugates for efficient targeting and synergistic suppression of breast cancer cell
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Zixuan
Chen, Keying
Lin, Tong
Zhao, Pengkai
Wang, Haixia
Su, Zhiqiang
coredata.dc:description: Breast cancer become the most prevalent malignancy and is rising up to the first leading cause of cancer-related mortality among the female people globally. Approaches for safe, specific targeting and efficient inhibition of breast cancer cells, are urgently demanded for lifting the survival rate of breast tumor-bearing patients. Herein, a versatile hydrogel drug carrier consisting of doxorubicin (DOX)-loaded hydrogel microsphere and carbon dots (CDs)-loaded peptide nanowires (DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs) was constructed, presenting specific targeting and synergistic anti-cancer cell potencies. The hydrogel PEG microspheres with a rich porous structure benefited the drug loading efficiency, demonstrating good biodegradability and higher drug release efficiency at acidic environment. Benefiting from the RGD group of the peptide nanowires, the drug-loaded system specific targeted onto the MDA-MB-231 cells, subsequently promoting the synergistic inhibition of the cancer cell growth by DOX and metformin derived CDs. This all-in-one smart drug carrier post a promising strategy for breast cancer treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyethylene glycol
Hydrogel microspheres
Drug carriers
Breast cancer cell
Targeted delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological characterization of PEG hydrogel microspheres
Structural characterization of PEG hydrogel microspheres
Optical investigation of hydrogel PEG microspheres
Drug loading and release from hydrogel PEG microspheres

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51873016, 32001012), and the Science and Technology Program of Guangzhou (202102010217).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological analysis of hydrogel microsphere components. (A) The formation mechanism of hydrogel microspheres. (B) Sequence diagram of PNFs and formation mechanism of PNFs-CDs. (C, D) SEM image of PEG hydrogel microspheres and the corresponding size distribution. (E, F) TEM image of CDs and the size distribution. (G) SEM image of PNFs. (H) TEM image of PNFs-CDs. (I-L) AFM images and the height distributions of PNFs and PNFs-CDs.

Structural analysis of DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs. (A) TEM images of PEG/PNFs-CDs along with local magnification. (B) Thermogravimetric curves of PEG hydrogel microspheres before and after drug loading. (C-D) XPS spectra of PEG/PNFs-CDs. (E-F) XPS spectra of DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs. (G) FT-IR spectra of CDs, PNFs, and PNFs-CDs. (H) FT-IR spectra of PEG/PNFs-CDs and DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs.

(A-B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and PNFs-CDs, and the corresponding images of the samples under UV light. (C-D) Fluorescence emission spectra of DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs, Cy5.5, PNFs-Cy5.5 and PEG/PNFs-Cy5.5, respectively. (E) Degradation efficiency of PEG/PNFs-CDs hydrogel microspheres on the 21st day in PBS buffer at different pH. (F) Drug release profiles of PEG/PNFs-CDs loaded with DOX at pH=6.6 and pH=7.4.

Investigation of the cellular targeting capability of the carriers. (A) Fluorescence imaging of the cellular uptake of PEG/PNFs1 and PEG/PNFs2 by MDA-MB-231 cells. (B-C) Flow cytometry analysis of MDA-MB-231 cells treated with PBS, PEG/PNFs1 and PEG/PNFs2, respectively.

Evaluation of the biocompatibility of the carriers and the anti-cancer cell effect of the drug-loaded carriers. (A-B) Fluorescence imaging of the live/dead staining and the corresponding cell viability of the MDA-MB-231 cells after treatment with various carriers. (C-D) Fluorescence imaging of the live/dead staining (DOX, 5 µg/mL) and the corresponding cell viability of the MDA-MB-231 cells after treatment with various DOX-loaded carriers at different concentrations of 0, 5, 10, 20 μg/mL DOX.

head.ce:title: Versatile hydrogel drug carrier loading with peptide-carbon dots conjugates for efficient targeting and synergistic suppression of breast cancer cell

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Polymer Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Polymer Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Polymer Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Nanomedicine, The Third Affiliated Hospital, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510630, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Nanomedicine, The Third Affiliated Hospital, Sun Yat-sen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Nanomedicine, The Third Affiliated Hospital, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510630, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Breast cancer become the most prevalent malignancy and is rising up to the first leading cause of cancer-related mortality among the female people globally. Approaches for safe, specific targeting and efficient inhibition of breast cancer cells, are urgently demanded for lifting the survival rate of breast tumor-bearing patients. Herein, a versatile hydrogel drug carrier consisting of doxorubicin (DOX)-loaded hydrogel microsphere and carbon dots (CDs)-loaded peptide nanowires (DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs) was constructed, presenting specific targeting and synergistic anti-cancer cell potencies. The hydrogel PEG microspheres with a rich porous structure benefited the drug loading efficiency, demonstrating good biodegradability and higher drug release efficiency at acidic environment. Benefiting from the RGD group of the peptide nanowires, the drug-loaded system specific targeted onto the MDA-MB-231 cells, subsequently promoting the synergistic inhibition of the cancer cell growth by DOX and metformin derived CDs. This all-in-one smart drug carrier post a promising strategy for breast cancer treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer become one of the most prevalent malignant tumors worldwide, and is climbing up to be the first leading cause of cancer-related death among the female population [1,2]. In 2020, roughly 2.3 million new breast cancer cases and 0.7 million cases of breast cancer-related death globally [1,2]. Therefore, there is a desperate need for early detection and effective treatment strategies [3,4]. Surgical resection is the most commonly used method for removing the solid malignant tumor, but the high rate of recurrence of breast cancer remains clinically fatal [5,6]. To enhance the therapeutic effect and inhibit the tumor recurrence, postoperative adjuvant therapy, such as radiotherapy, is recommended [5]. However, the traditional radiotherapy presents the lack of specific treatment due to unspecific targeting, which damage the healthy tissues while destroying the residual or recurrent tumor cells. As a consequence, more effective strategies for safely facilitating the elimination of the breast tumor cells are urgently demanded.

Pharmacotherapy is one of the indispensable treatments for breast cancer. Especially, the chemotherapeutical administration is executed in the preoperative and postoperative therapy. In general, there are insufficient drugs can be delivered into the tumor site when the administration of drugs with low dosage. However, systemic toxicity occurred when high dosage of drugs was applied [7]. Therefore, the drug carriers have attracted widespread attention in recent years [8–11]. However, the transportation of the drugs into the desired location by carriers remains far below expectation, which has been revealed that the rapidly formed protein corona [12,13] facilitates the clearness of the drug-loaded carriers by immune cells [14,15]. Polyethylene glycol (PEG) has been applied to resist the adsorption of the protein corona or attracted the stealth proteins [16–19], which will prolong the circulation of the carriers in the biofluid and subsequently improve the delivery efficiency of the drugs into the tumor site [20,21]. Diverse PEG decorated vectors are extensively investigated, but the high drug loading efficiency and sustained drug release profile are necessary to be optimized. Hybrid PEG microspheres, presenting sustainable drug delivery capability, are promising intravenously injectable drug carriers with minimal invasion [22].

Engineered drug carriers with targeting ligands can further enhance the drug delivery efficiency [4], which subsequently improve the specific anti-tumor effect and significantly reduced systemic toxicity. It was found that integrin αvβ3 shows high expression on the cell membrane of most tumor cells, such as cervical cancer, bladder cancer, breast cancer et al., playing an important role in tumor proliferation, migration and invasion as well as in the process of tumor revascularization [23–25]. Therefore, integrin αvβ3 is commonly recognized as a target for the guidance of designing the drug carriers to improve the targeted cancer or disease therapy [26–29]. The tripeptide Arg–Gly–Asp, RGD, is well known as the functional ligand to specifically target the integrin αvβ3, which has been comprehensively investigated and achieved good therapeutic results in the in vitro and in vivo cancer treatment studies [30,31].

In this work, to elevate the anti-cancer cell efficiency, a hybrid hydrogel drug carrier was fabricated, comprising DOX-loaded hydrogel PEG microsphere and CDs-loaded peptide nanowires with remarkable targeting potency and synergistic inhibition of cancer cells. The hydrogel PEG microspheres possessed porous structure for improving the drug loading efficiency, biodegradability, and sustained drug release manner. The self-assembled peptide nanowires with a functional peptide sequence of RGD-AEAKAEAK-YWYAF-CCY-RGD, where the part of CCY was able to bind onto hydrogel PEG microspheres and the sequence of YWYAF benefited the adsorption of carbon dots. In addition, the RGD group of the peptide nanowires enhanced the specific targeting onto the breast cancer cells, MDA-MB-231 cells. Upon targeting onto MDA-MB-231 cells, the carrier loaded DOX and CDs derived from metformin presented synergistic suppression of the cancer cell growth, which indicated that the hydrogel drug carrier posts a prospective strategy for breast cancer treatment.

8 arm PEG acrylate (8-PEG, MW=20,000) was purchased from JenKem (Beijing, China). Ammonia, hexane, dimethylsilicone oil, metformin, citric acid, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) were purchased from J&K (Beijing, China). Cyanine 5.5 carboxylic acid (Cy5.5) was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). The peptide (RGD-AEAKAEAK-YWYAF-CCY-RGD) was provided by BankPeptide (Hefei, China). All of the other chemicals in the experiment were directly used without further purification.

8-PEG was used as the monomer for fabricating hydrogel microspheres, where the Michael addition reaction was carried out by the reaction of ammonia with the double bond of acrylate in the 8-PEG. Hydrogel microspheres were obtained by emulsion polymerization [32]. Specifically, 75 mg of 8-PEG was dissolved in 0.3 mL of ultrapure water, and the gel pre-solution was formed after homogenization by sonication. 15 mL of dimethylsilicone oil with emulsifier was stirred for 30 min, which was then slowly dropped into the gel pre-solution for 30 min. Subsequently, 400 μL of ammonia was added and stirred for 30 min, and the emulsion containing gel microspheres was obtained after standing. They were purified by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm/min for 10 min, followed by redispersing in an equal amount of hexane. Upon repeatedly centrifugation and washing with ethanol and water, respectively, the hydrogel PEG microspheres were obtained after lyophilization.

The appropriate amount of ethanol can mediate the conformational transition of linear peptide molecules to β-folded structures, which can then self-assemble and aggregate into homogeneous peptide nanowires (PNFs) [33]. Specifically, 1 mg of peptide was dissolved in absolute ethanol solution to form 0.05 mg/mL of peptide solution, and the peptide nanowire was self-assembled in a 20 % ethanol solution heated in a water bath at 37 °C for 5 days. The obtained peptide nanowire solution was stored at 4 °C for further use.

Carboxy-functionalized Cy5.5 dye-linked nanowires were used for cell targeting experiment. Briefly, 2 mg of Cy5.5-COOH was dissolved in anhydrous DMF to acquire a dye stock solution with a concentration of 10 mg/mL. After that, 10 μL of Cy5.5-COOH and N-ethyl-N′-[3-dimethylaminopropyl] carbodiimide (EDC) was added into 1 mL of peptide solution, which was placed in a shaker at 150 rpm/min and 27 ℃ overnight to fully perform the reaction. The obtained mixture was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO=3500 Da) with deionized water for 1 day to remove the unlinked dye components, and the PNFs-Cy5.5 was obtained and stored at 4 ℃ in the dark.

Another peptide with the different sequence of AEAKAEAK-YWYAF-CCY was designed. The peptide nanowires were synthesized in the same way as described above, and the Cy5.5-stained nanowires were synthesized as a control group for the cell targeting experiments.

Carbon dots (CDs) are produced by hydrothermal method with metformin and citric acid [34]. Specifically, 0.5 g of metformin and 0.5 g of citric acid were dissolved into 10 mL of ultrapure water and reacted in a hydrothermal reaction kettle in a blast oven at 160 ℃ for 10 h. The larger impurities were filtered out using a 0.22 µm polyethersulfone membrane, and the small molecule impurities were removed by dialysis in ultrapure water using a dialysis bag (MWCO = 3500 Da) to obtain the carbon dots solution, which was stored at 4 °C under light-proof conditions.

The YWYAF sequence in PNFs was used to link PNFs and CDs via π-π stacking interaction between their benzene rings [33]. Specifically, 0.5 mL of CDs solution, 1 mL of 80 % hydrazine hydrate solution, and 0.5 mL of PNFs solution were well mixed on a shaker (150 rpm/min). Subsequently, the mixture was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (MWCO = 3500 Da) for 24 h. Ultrapure water was refreshed for dialysis three times to remove excess CDs and hydrazine hydrate. Finally, the purified PNFs-CDs nanohybrid solution was stored in the dark at 4 °C for further use.

1 mL of PNFs-CDs nanohybrid solution and 10 μL of photo initiator (DMPA) were added dropwise into the emulsion mixture containing hydrogel microspheres while maintaining stirring for 30 min, which was subsequently irradiated under a 365 nm UV curing lamp for 30 min. After centrifugation at 10,000 rpm/min for 10 min, the oil phase was carefully poured out and then the mixture was centrifuged again under the same conditions upon redispersed in an equal amount of hexane. The obtained solids were washed with ethanol and water, respectively, and the final PEG/PNFs-CDs drug carriers were obtained after lyophilization.

First, 10 mg/mL of DOX solution was prepared to form a pre-solution of drug-loaded gel. 75 mg of 8-PEG and 0.2 mL of DOX solution (10 mg/mL) were sonicated well to obtain a pre-solution of gel. Next, drug-loaded hydrogel microspheres were prepared according to the above-described steps, which were attached by PNFs-CDs to assemble DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs microspheres.

Morphological characterization: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a microscope (Talos F200X, FEI, USA) at 30 KeV. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained by scanning the sample through a microscope (Gemini 300, ZEISS, Germany) after gold spraying at 10 KV. Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) images were obtained in an AFM microscope (MultiMode 8, Bruker, Germany) at 1 Hz after fixing the sample piece on a silicon wafer.

Structural analysis: Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed on a spectrometer (Nicolet 8700, Thermo-Fisher, USA) to analyze the functional groups of the components of the PEG/PNFs-CDs carrier system, and the dried microsphere samples were ground and pressed with KBr in the range of 400–40000 cm-1. XPS tests of PEG/PNFs-CDs and DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs were carried out using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (UX-2500MA, EDAX, USA) to compare the elemental changes in the obtained products.

Fluorescence analysis: Fluorescence spectra of CDs, PNFs-CDs, PEG/PNFs-CDs and DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs were performed using an LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). To determine the Cy5.5 staining efficiency, the fluorescence spectra of the Cy5.5, PNFs-Cy5.5 and PEG/PNFs-Cy5.5 were performed at an excitation wavelength of 680 nm.

Degradation performance analysis: PBS buffer was used in the degradation experiments to simulate the physiological environment and samples were examined periodically at 1 d, 4 d, 7 d, 10 d, 16 d and 21 d using an optical microscope (CKX41, OLYMPUS, Japan). The degradation carriers with the hydrogel microspheres were also evaluated quantitatively by mass changes.

DOX in PBS buffer solutions with 0.01 mg/mL, 0.015 mg/mL, 0.02 mg/mL, 0.025 mg/mL, 0.03 mg/mL, 0.04 mg/mL and 0.05 mg/mL were accurately configured. The absorbance (A) of the samples at 480 nm with different concentrations (C) was measured by UV spectrophotometer. The standard regression curve of DOX in PBS was obtained by repeating the measurement for three times. The standard concentration curve measured above was A = 0.1554×C+0.006, where A is the absorbance of the solution at 480 nm and C (μg/mL) is the concentration of the drug in the solution.

10 mg of the dried microsphere product were dispersed into 10 mL of DMSO solution, upon stirring at 37 ℃ for 24 h to dissolve the DOX. The supernatant after centrifugation was collected for determine the drug concentration via measuring the UV absorbance at 480 nm. The measurement was repeated three times to obtain the drug loading efficiency (%), which was calculated as follows:
(1)
Drug loading
%
=
Drug concentration of supernatant
mg / mL
× 10 mL
Initial drug mass
mg
× 100 %

10 mg of dried microspheres of each sample (DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs, DOX@PEG/PNFs, DOX@PEG/CDs) in PBS buffer solution (4 mL) at pH=7.4 and pH=6.6, respectively, were transferred into dialysis bags. They were placed into beakers containing 80 mL of the same medium, kept in a water bath at 37 °C. 4 mL of the solution in the beaker outside the dialysis bag was quickly extracted at 0.5 h, 1 h, 1.5 h, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h, 10 h, 12 h on the first day and once a day for the next 12 days, while 4 mL of the fresh medium was correspondingly added to the solution. The absorbance of the obtained drug-containing solution was measured at 480 nm, which was applied to calculated the drug release at predetermined time points. The drug release efficiency can be described as follows (Eq. 2), where m0 is the amount of the initial loaded drugs, Vi= 4 mL and V0= 80 mL.
(2)
Drug release ( % ) =
V
i
∑
1
n − 1
C
i
+
V
0
C
n
m
0
× 100 %

PNFs (containing RGD)-Cy5.5 (PNFs1) and PNFs (without RGD)-Cy5.5 (PNFs2) were linked to PEG hydrogel microspheres for targeting experiments. PEG/PNFs1 and PEG/PNFs2 were added into the wells of the plate at 100 μg/mL and incubated with the MDA-MB-231cells in serum-free medium for 12 h. Afterwards, the medium containing the free microspheres was removed and the cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 10 min. Subsequently, the fixed cells were washed with PBS and stained with DAPI for imaging by the inverted fluorescence microscope.

In addition, flow cytometric analysis was performed to assess the targeting capability of PEG/PNFs1 and PEG/PNFs2 onto MDA-MB-231. The cells were incubated with 100 μg/mL of PEG/PNFs1 and PEG/PNFs2 as described above, respectively, afterwards, the mean fluorescence intensity of the Cy5.5 signal on each cell was analyzed by flow cytometry.

Heat-sterilized FBS (10 %, w/v) containing penicillin (100 U/mL) and streptomycin (0.1 mg/mL) was used for culturing MDA-MB-231. PBS, PEG/PNFs, PEG/CDs, and PEG/PNFs-CDs were co-cultured with MDA-MB-231 cells in 96-well cell culture plate at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well, respectively, and the cytotoxicity was evaluated using the CCK-8 kit (APExBIO, USA) and live/dead cell staining kit (Beyotime, China) analysis. Specifically, samples with 1 mg/mL were co-cultured with MDA-MB-231 cell and the control was added with PBS solution, the cells were cultured for 12 h. Upon washing the cells with PBS, CCK-8 reagent was added into each well. After 2 h incubation, the absorbance (OD) of the samples was measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader (Synergy H1, BioTek, USA) and the cell viability (%) was calculated compared to the control group. Similarly, after 12 h incubation, live/dead cell staining reagent was added to the plate and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min, afterwards, the cells were imaged using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Ti2-U, Nikon, Japan).

DOX@PEG/PNFs, DOX@PEG/CDs and DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs were co-cultured with MDA-MB-231 cells in 96-well plate at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well, respectively. The cytotoxicity analysis was performed by using CCK-8 kit (APExBIO, USA) and live/dead cell staining kit (Beyotime, China). Specifically, samples were configured at concentrations of 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL and 20 μg/mL according to the DOX content. 96-well plate containing the samples and cells were placed in the cell culture incubator for 12 h and cells were then washed with PBS. CCK-8 reagent was added and reacted in the wells for 2 h, and the absorbance (OD) of the samples was measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader (Synergy H1, BioTek, USA). The cell viability (%) was calculated according to the recorded absorbance compared to the control group. Similarly, after 12 h incubation, live/dead cell staining reagents were added to the wells and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. The live and dead cells stained with Calcein AM and Propidium Iodide (PI) were observed and imaged by using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Ti2-U, Nikon, Japan).

The statistical results were presented by mean ± standard error (SE), n = 3/group. Differences between groups were analyzed by using one-way ANOVA followed by Bonferroni's post hoc test. The statistical significance was defined as *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, and ****p < 0.0001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological characterization of PEG hydrogel microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: PEG hydrogel microspheres were prepared by Michael addition reaction of 8-PEG with ammonia based on emulsion polymerization method ( Fig. 1A). CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using citric acid as the carbon source and metformin as the nitrogen source. Meanwhile, experiments were carried out to synthesize PNFs by designing peptide sequences to complete the self-assembly reaction, and the nanowires have good size dispersion and can serve to connect CDs and hydrogel microspheres (Fig. 1B). The PEG microspheres were well dispersed, uniform in size and maintained good shape integrity with an average particle size of 6.66 ± 2.31 µm as observed by SEM imaging (Fig. 1C-D). The carbon dots were quasi-spherical nanoparticles with good dispersion and possessed a relatively well-developed crystal structure (Fig. 1D). The lattice can be clearly observed with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which is the (100) face of graphite, indicating that the nanoparticles have a graphitic microcrystalline structure and can therefore be referred to as carbon nanoparticles or carbon dots. The size distribution of the carbon dots recorded by TEM imaging was analyzed by using the Image J software (Fig. 1F). The results show that the size distribution of the carbon dots ranged from 1 to 5 nm with an average size of 2.48 nm resulting from 100 carbon dots via Gaussian fit, consistent with the results reported in the previous literature[34]. During the peptide self-assembly process, the morphology of the self-assembled peptide nanowires from the initial granular form to the subsequent nanowire state with the thickness of 2 nm (Fig. 1I and Fig. S1). It can be seen that the morphology of PNFs-CDs, nanowire bound with carbon dots, is uniform. The height of the binding site is roughly 6 nm, consistent with the thickness of the nanowire and the size of the carbon dots detected as described above, indicating the good binding (Fig. 1H & K).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of PEG hydrogel microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: Each component of the spherical carriers can be observed in the TEM image ( Fig. 2A). Nanowires can be detected on the surface of the microsphere and granular carbon dots are present on the nanowires. This provides evidence that the PNFs-CDs were successfully loaded onto the hydrogel microspheres. In addition, thermogravimetric analysis of the carriers before and after drug loading was carried out (Fig. 2B). The remaining mass of the PEG microspheres was about 5 % at 600 °C, while the remaining mass of the DOX loaded microspheres at 600 °C was only 2.5 %. The thermal stability of the microspheres was slightly reduced after loading with DOX. The inset plot is the result of the derivation of the thermogravimetric curve, indicating the thermal decomposition at around 230 ℃ is caused by the presence of DOX. The combined thermogravimetric data manifests that the slight influence of the loaded drug on the stability of the product was negligible.

The XPS energy spectra of PEG/PNFs-CDs is shown in Fig. 2C-D, where S presents in two forms, -SH and C-S-C, respectively. The group of -SH is the residue of the CCY in the peptide sequence that has not been completely reacted, and C-S-C is the chemical bond formed by the reaction of -SH on the peptide with the hydrogel microspheres, thus also demonstrating the successful preparation of PEG/PNFs-CDs. Compared to the XPS spectra of PEG/PNFs-CDs, the one of DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs shows a detectable increase in the proportion of N atoms due to the presence of -NH2 in the additional DOX (Fig. 2E-F).

The appearance of the phenyl ether bond as well as the disappearance of -CH2- on the FT-IR spectrum of PNFs-CDs, indicating the successful linkage of CDs to PNFs (Fig. 2G). By comparing the FT-IR spectra of the PEG hydrogel microspheres (Fig. S2) and PEG/PNFs-CDs, it was obvious that the peaks of both PEG and PNFs-CDs were detected in the spectrum of PEG/PNFs-CDs, further demonstrating the successful linkage of these two components. The successful loading of DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs is further evidenced by the appearance of phenolic peaks, as well as the aliphatic ether peaks became more numerous (Fig. 2H).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical investigation of hydrogel PEG microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption peak of the carbon dots is around 340 nm according to the UV absorption spectrum in Fig. S3. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and PNFs-CDs were both recorded with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm, implying the combination of PNFs and CDs ( Fig. 3A-B). A typical fluorescence emission spectrum of DOX was detected when the DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs was excited by 480 nm, indicating the successful loading of DOX into PEG/PNFs-CDs (Fig. 3 C).

In order to investigate the targeting properties of the drug carriers, fluorescent PNFs-Cy5.5 was synthesized. Fluorescence emission spectra of Cy5.5，PNFs-Cy5.5 and PEG/PNFs-Cy5.5 were performed with the excitation wavelength of 680 nm as shown in Fig. 3D. The typical fluorescence peak of Cy5.5 at 710 nm was observed in the spectra of PNFs-Cy5.5 as well as PEG/PNFs-Cy5.5, demonstrating the Cy5.5 has been successfully linked into the composite nanomaterials, which can be used as fluorescent probes for assessment the targeting capability of the nanomaterials to cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and release from hydrogel PEG microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: The degradation properties of injectable biomaterials are critical for in vivo therapeutic applications. Fig. S4 shows the changes in degradation of PEG hydrogels in PBS buffers at pH=6.6, pH=7.4 and pH=8. Within three weeks of incubation, the microspheres were gradually degraded. As shown in the bright field, the number of the microspheres got less along time and the spherical shape of the microspheres become blurred until completely degraded at longer period of time such as day 21. The degradation efficiency was obtained by evaluating the remaining mass of the product at the 21st day, which was to be greater than 80 % at all the three pH (Fig. 3E). As a consequence, the hydrogel microspheres present potential time-dependent degradation performance when used as an injectable drug carrier.

A standard calibration curve of absorbance versus DOX concentration was performed in PBS solution, which was fitted for the evaluation of the release behavior of DOX from the carriers (Fig. S5). The drug loading efficiency of the PEG/PNFs-CDs hydrogel carrier was obtained as 90 % based on the standard curve. Apparently, the DOX release profiles were pH-dependent as shown in Fig. 3 F, with the faster release rate at pH=6.6 compared to pH=7.4, which was also noted in the previous work[35]. After monitoring the DOX release behaviors for 12 days, it can be seen that the release rate was relatively faster in the first day and gradually slowed down in the following days. The DOX release efficiency approached to 38.8 % and 27 % at pH=6.6 and pH=7.4, respectively. DOX can be protonated under acidic conditions, resulting in better hydrophilicity and the subsequent faster release from the PEG/PNFs-CDs hydrogel carrier. This demonstrates that the drug carriers possess the promising potential for effectively delivering the drugs into tumor environment.

The αvβ3 receptor is overexpressed in most of cancer cells such as MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells (Fig. S6), which can be specifically recognized by the peptide RGD[31]. The designed peptide containing the RGD sequence, linked to hydrogel microspheres upon assembling into nanowires (PEG/PNFs1). To assess the targeting capacity of the designed PEG/PNFs1 to MDA-MB-231 cells, the peptide without RGD sequence was applied to assemble the carrier, PEG/PNFs2, for comparison. Cy5.5 labeled PEG/PNFs1 and PEG/PNFs2 were cultured respectively with cancer cells and the targeting efficiency was evaluated by the fluorescence imaging and flow cytometry.

Compared to the PEG/PNFs2 group, the fluorescence intensity of Cy5.5 labeled PEG/PNFs1 with RGD sequence was significantly higher in the cytoplasm of the cells as shown in Fig. 4A. It is obvious that PEG/PNFs1 were able to target the MDA-MB-231 cells due to the RGD sequence, therefore enhancing the cellular uptake of the designed carriers. We further evaluated the targeting capability of the carriers on 4T1 cells, which also showed the RGD sequence benefited the targeting (Fig. S7). In addition, flow cytometry was applied to statically analysis of the targeting capability of PEG/PNFs1 based on the mean fluorescence intensity of the Cy5.5 labeled carriers inside each cell. Compared to the PEG/PNFs2 group, the percentage of the cells with Cy5.5 signals as well as the mean intracellular fluorescence intensity were apparently higher in the PEG/PNFs1 group (Fig. 4B-C), consistent with the result observed in the fluorescence imaging, demonstrating the higher targeting capability of the designed drug carrier onto MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells.

To determine the feasibility of drug carriers for breast cancer treatment, the cell viability was firstly assessed upon treatment with the carriers via live/dead cell staining and CCK-8 kit assay ( Fig. 5A-B). After incubation the cells with 1 mg/mL PEG/PNFs, PEG/CDs, and PEG/PNFs-CDs for 12 h (1xPBS as blank control), respectively, the cells were stained with Calcein AM (Green channel) and PI (Red channel) to distinguish the live and dead cells. According to the fluorescent images, almost all the cells were stained with Calcein AM in blank control and PEG/PNFs groups, indicating the excellent biocompatibility of the carriers. However, besides the majority of the Calcein AM-stained live cells, a few PI-stained dead cells can be observed in PEG/CDs and PEG/PNFs-CDs groups, implying the slight cytotoxicity in the presence of CDs. Additionally, the biocompatibility of the various carriers was further statically analysis by CCK-8 kit assay as shown in Fig. 5B. The cell viability of the cells treated with only PBS was set as the control group, with the value of 100 %. Obviously, the cells treated with PEG/PNFs presented similar cell viability compared to the control group, manifesting the good biocompatibility. The cells treated with PEG/CDs and PEG/PNFs-CDs showed a relatively decreased viability, consistent with the result derived from the fluorescence imaging. Consequently, the hybrid carrier of PEG/PNFs presented a good biocompatibility, while carriers with the metformin derived CDs possessed a slight cytotoxicity, implying the potential in the inhibition of the cancer cell growth.

To enhance the cytotoxicity of the carriers, the classical chemotherapeutic drug, DOX, was applied to further suppress the growth of the cancer cells. DOX was loaded into the hydrogel microspheres, to obtain the drug-loaded carriers, DOX@PEG/PNFs, DOX@PEG/CDs and DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs. Upon treatment with the drug-loaded carriers for 12 h, the live and dead cells were stained with Calcein AM and PI, respectively. Apparently, the released DOX facilitate the apoptosis of the cancer cells, as dead cells stained with PI could be clearly seen in the red channel of the fluorescent images in DOX@PEG/PNFs and DOX@PEG/CDs groups (Fig. 5C). Benefiting from

the targeting capability of RGD in PNFs onto cancer cells and the synergistic cytotoxicity of CDs and DOX, more dead cells were detected among DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs treated cells. In addition, CCK-8 assay was applied to statically evaluate the cell viability after treatment with these drug-loaded carriers at predetermined DOX concentrations. It can be found that cell viability decreased along with increasing the DOX concentration in all groups, with the most significant drop into 29.3 % at 20 μg/mL DOX in DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs group (Fig. 5D). As a consequence, consistent with the conclusion from the fluorescence imaging, the anti-cancer cell effect was obviously detected upon the cells treated with DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs, indicating the promising potential of the designed drug-loaded carrier in anti-tumor effect.

In conclusion, an assembled versatile hydrogel drug carrier consisted of DOX-loaded hydrogel microsphere and CDs-loaded peptide nanowires, possessing specific targeting capability and synergistic cytotoxicity to breast cancer cells. The hydrogel PEG microspheres were prepared by emulsion polymerization, and the CDs and self-assembled peptide nanowires were derived from metformin and functional peptide with pre-determined sequence, respectively. The successful preparation of PEG/PNFs-CDs drug carriers was verified by morphological and structural characterizations via TEM and AFM imaging, and XPS and FTIR spectra. The hydrogel carriers showed excellent degradability, biocompatibility, and specific targeting to MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells. Benefiting from the high porosity of the hydrogel microspheres, the drug loading efficiency was up to 90 %. The release efficiency in the cancer environment (pH=6.6) was 38.8 %, which was relatively higher than the one at the physiological pH of 7.4. Upon targeting onto the MDA-MB-231 cells via the interaction of RGD group and αvβ3 receptor of the cell membrane, DOX and metformin derived CDs released from the DOX@PEG/PNFs-CDs presented great synergistic inhibition of the cancer cell growth. The designed and assembled hydrogel drug carrier with degradability, biocompatibility, and targeting capability loading with chemotherapeutic drugs providing a promising strategy of constructing all-in-one smart drug carriers for enhancing inhibition of cancer cells.

Zhiqiang Su: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization. Pengkai Zhao: Investigation. Haixia Wang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Keying Chen: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tong Lin: Investigation. Zixuan Xiao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51873016, 32001012), and the Science and Technology Program of Guangzhou (202102010217).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2024.110404.
